title: Portfolio hierarchy & scoping — synthesis + Vector recommendations
topic: Aggregator: synthesize R022-R027 into adoption recommendations for Vector
summary: Cross-tool synthesis of hierarchy + scoping models with concrete adoption recommendations for Vector
content:
Contents
  1. 1. Executive Summary
  2. 2. Hierarchy Model — Cross-Tool Comparison
  3. 3. Scoping Containers — Cross-Tool Comparison
  4. 4. What Vector Already Does Right
  5. 5. What Vector Does Better Than the Field
  6. 6. Gaps — What Industry Leaders Have That Vector Lacks
  7. 7. Recommendations for Market-Leading Vector
  8. 8. Open Architectural Decisions — Resolved
  9. 9. Sources

1. Executive Summary

This paper synthesises the prior six papers (R022 Rally hierarchy, R023 Rally projects/scoping, R024 Targetprocess, R025 GitLab, R026 Jira, R027 Azure DevOps) into a single comparative view and a concrete adoption playbook for Vector. Vector has already chosen a strict-tree-by-construction model — single-table self-referential FK on user_stories.parent_portfolio_item_id and portfolio_items.hierarchy_parent; a hard chain Theme → Initiative → Feature → User Story → Task; defects, user stories, and epic stories attach only to Features; cycles are structurally impossible because each child type has exactly one legal parent type; subtree traversal uses a recursive CTE; rollups are async with a last_rollup_at reconciliation timestamp.

Measured against the field, this design lands Vector squarely in the median-to-best zone for hierarchy correctness. Rally, GitLab, Jira, and Azure DevOps all permit orphans, level-skipping, or cross-type parenting that produces drift over time; only Targetprocess approaches Vector's strictness, and it does so with separate-table-per-type rather than a self-referential single table. Vector's principal gaps are not in correctness but in UX surface area: cross-container scoping, drag-to-reparent with a type-aware drop predicate, hierarchy-band filtering on backlog views, and a sandboxed plan-style what-if workspace. The recommendations in §7 close those gaps without compromising the strict-tree invariant.

On the three open architectural decisions (§8): we recommend Path C (global tree, container-clamped at view time), with the container named Workspace (matching Aha! and Linear and avoiding the user's explicitly disliked "Project" term), and clamp at any level via a tenant-configurable per-Workspace minimum-attach-level setting that gadmin sets once per Workspace.

Path C clarified (C.2). The Workspace container is itself a tree, not a flat tag — schema mirrors the existing portfolio_items.hierarchy_parent pattern via an org_nodes table with self-referential parent_id. Real organisations (Lloyds-scale: Group → Division → Sub-division → Department → Team → Squad, 1,000–3,000 nodes) need arbitrary depth and width, tenant-named levels, and per-node clamp policy. Org Design is a first-class product surface — its own page with a canvas-based block-diagram editor, not a hidden settings list. Working name "Org Design" and the node noun (candidates: Unit, Group, Branch, Node, or gadmin-named) remain open. Detail in §8.

2. Hierarchy Model — Cross-Tool Comparison

The eight hierarchy dimensions covered consistently across R022–R027 are: schema model, level naming and count, parent-link mechanism, rollup mechanics, reparenting behaviour, orphan handling, subtree filtering, and custom-level support. Vector is added as the seventh column.

Dimension Rally Targetprocess GitLab Jira Azure DevOps Vector
Schema / storage Single PortfolioItem base + slashed sub-type endpoints; story is separate HierarchicalRequirement Per-type tables / endpoints (separate UserStories, Features, Epics, PortfolioEpics) Single issues table (STI) for all WorkItems; issue_type discriminator; parent-child in separate work_item_parent_links table Single Issue table; type field; parent_id column Single work-item store; type field; parent stored as System.LinkTypes.Hierarchy link record (not a column) Two tables: portfolio_items (Theme/Initiative/Feature) self-referential FK + user_stories with FK to portfolio item; defects/epic-stories on user_stories
Score 6/10 3/10 8/10 7/10 5/10 9/10
Level naming & count (default) Theme, Initiative, Feature (configurable up to 5: P1–P5) Portfolio Epic, Epic, Feature, User Story, Task/Bug (5 fixed levels) Epic (group), Child Epic (Ultimate, up to 7 deep), Issue, Task Subtask (-1), Standard (0), Epic (1); Premium adds Initiative+ above Epic, unlimited custom levels Epic, Feature, User Story / PBI / Requirement, Task; Bug peer of Story; up to 5 portfolio levels above requirement backlog Theme, Initiative, Feature, User Story, Task (5 fixed; defect/epic-story attach to Feature)
Score 6/10 5/10 6/10 8/10 7/10 5/10
Parent-link mechanism Single Parent FK on each PortfolioItem; story has single PortfolioItem FK to lowest level Direct single-value FK column to next type up (Feature.Id on Story, Epic.Id on Feature) Link-table row in work_item_parent_links; single-parent enforced by adding-new-link-removes-old Single parent_id column on Issue; unified from legacy epic-link + parent-link Tree-topology link record in generic link store; one of many link types alongside Related/Predecessor/etc. Single self-referential FK column per table (hierarchy_parent on portfolio_items, parent_portfolio_item_id on user_stories); type-strict by construction
Score 7/10 6/10 6/10 8/10 4/10 9/10
Rollup compute model Stored on write, asynchronous; LastRollupDate; manual "correct rollup discrepancy" affordance Computed on read; Progress = SUM(EffortCompleted) / SUM(Effort); Bug effort excluded Computed on read in epic header / roadmap; descendant count, total weight, progress % Computed on read in Plans; story-point sum from child issues; not stored separately Computed by Analytics service (eventually consistent); not trigger-maintained; rollup display latency on edit Async stored rollups with last_rollup_at reconciliation (matches Rally + Azure model, beats Jira/Targetprocess on display latency for large trees)
Score 8/10 4/10 5/10 5/10 6/10 8/10
Reparenting Cuts old chain, creates new sub-hierarchy; lock-down work rules can disable; cross-project allowed within Workspace Story→Feature edit; cross-project allowed; Feature→new Project pulls child Stories that originally shared the Project; Epic→new Project does NOT carry children (creates orphans) Drag-and-drop in epic child-items UI (Ultimate); subtree follows; single-parent constraint Edit Parent field; subtasks cannot cross project boundary; Epic→Story conversion orphans children Drag-reparent on backlog Parents view; multi-select Change Parent; respects category rules; cross-project parent links possible after move Subtree-moves-as-unit by design (recursive CTE descends, all descendants follow); type-aware drop predicate from registry blocks invalid drops
Score 6/10 4/10 7/10 4/10 7/10 9/10
Orphan handling Permitted; can be required at workspace level via opt-in; canonical query Parent=null AND PortfolioItem=null Permitted but flagged; orphans documented as a recovery problem after cross-project Epic moves; recommends custom filter views Permitted; orphans appear ungrouped in backlog; warnings filter in Plans Permitted; unparented items appear in backlog; new hierarchy levels do NOT retroactively parent existing items (creates orphans) Permitted and surfaced; "Unparented backlog items" section on hierarchical backlog Allowed at every level — items can be created as orphans (no parent) to support backlog ingestion before triage. UI flags them with an "orphaned" marker so they remain visible and assignable, but creation is never blocked
Score 4/10 3/10 4/10 3/10 5/10 9/10
Subtree filter UX Portfolio Kanban (level pick) + Tree Grid + community apps for deep export Board / List / Timeline; Advanced DSL with dot-notation (?Feature.Epic is 'X') Roadmap (Gantt) with expand-rows; "filter roadmap to this epic's descendants" action; Epic-tree view inside epic page Plans hierarchy-range filter (From/To level); JQL portfolioChildIssuesOf(); Plan source scoping Hierarchical backlog view with Parents toggle; Mapping pane; tree-of-work-items WIQL query; Area Path orthogonal filter Recursive CTE backed; renderer can clamp to From/To level; needs UX (see §6 gap)
Score 6/10 7/10 7/10 8/10 7/10 4/10
Custom level / type support Workspace admins rename/add/delete up to 5 levels; new types create new WSAPI endpoints; no migration Rename only; cannot add new types or new levels; service desk request open Custom WIT roadmap exists but not GA; type framework defined in Ruby per-type classes Premium/Enterprise: unlimited custom levels above Epic; cannot insert between existing; site-wide Inheritance process: up to 5 portfolio levels stacked on top; cannot insert between; rename allowed Today: 5 fixed levels, no custom types; future tenant-configurable container model is a possible 4th dimension (gadmin-level)
Score 7/10 3/10 3/10 6/10 6/10 2/10

3. Scoping Containers — Cross-Tool Comparison

The two scoping dimensions covered across R023–R027 are: (a) what each tool calls its primary scoping container and how it nests, and (b) how containers carry permissions, work items, and movement semantics. The "Project" term is used by half the field and rejected by the other half on deliberate UX grounds.

Dimension Rally Targetprocess GitLab Jira Azure DevOps Vector (proposed)
Container name Project (under Workspace, under Subscription); also called "Team" interchangeably in docs Project (sometimes labelled "Portfolio"); + Program as flat upward grouping tag Group + Subgroup + Project (5-tier with experimental Organization above) Project; flat Project Categories as labels (no nesting); Plans as cross-project planning sandbox Project (under Organization); + Area Path tree (up to 14 levels deep) as orthogonal axis inside a Project Workspace (recommended; avoids "Project" connotation; matches Aha! + Linear conventions)
Nesting depth Project nests via Parent Team FK at unbounded depth under one Workspace Programs do NOT nest; Projects do NOT nest; one flat tier each Subgroup tree up to 20 levels deep; Projects can sit at any level Projects do NOT nest; categories are flat Projects do NOT nest; Area Paths nest up to 14 levels INSIDE a Project Workspace nesting via self-referential FK on workspace.parent_workspace_id (recommended; subgroup-style)
Permission model Per-Project ProjectPermission (Viewer/Editor/Project Admin); Workspace User baseline; Subscription Admin top 4 account types × per-membership Role; intersection of project + team membership; no row-level beyond membership 7 default roles inherited parent→child; visibility cascade rule (child ≤ parent); minimum-access role for visibility-without-project-access 3 layers: global / project permission scheme / issue security scheme (row-level); team-managed (open/limited/private) vs company-managed (35+ permissions) Permissions scoped to Area Path nodes (not parent links); team binding to Area Path(s); access tiers Stakeholder/Basic/Basic+TestPlans Per-Workspace role + cascade to descendants (GitLab pattern); row-level via existing tenant_id isolation; gadmin override at top
What lives in container Stories, defects, tasks, iterations, releases, milestones — but PortfolioItems float at Workspace level above All work items (Stories, Tasks, Bugs, Features, Epics, Portfolio Epics) require Project FK; Programs/Teams/Users global Issues/MRs/CI in Project; Epics at Group; Roadmaps/Iterations at Group All issues belong to exactly one Project (hard NOT NULL); workflows/screens/components per-project Work items, repos, pipelines, test plans, wikis — heavyweight container; Microsoft recommends single-Project + Area Paths Open question — see §8 (Path C recommended: tree global, items carry workspace_id, view-time clamp)
Cross-container item movement Cannot delete Projects (only Close); changing Project field on locked items is the one allowed edit; cross-project work allowed within Workspace Story→new Project edits Project FK; cross-project parent links permitted; Epic move orphans children Issues move between projects via Move; transfer subgroups/projects under different parent group; visibility cascade re-checked Move Issues changes key prefix; cross-project parent allowed in company-managed only; subtasks always same-project Move work item across Projects within org; ID preserved; revision preserved; child links remain (creating cross-project parent edges) Subtree-moves-as-unit on Workspace reparent; type-aware predicate validates; recursive CTE rewrites workspace_id for entire descendant set
Strategy items above container PortfolioItems at Workspace level — span all Projects under it (deliberate strategy/execution decoupling) Portfolio Epics at Project level (no level above container) Epics at Group level — span all descendant subgroups + projects Plans (Premium) span unlimited Projects — sandbox layer above Project boundary Cross-project queries supported; cross-project tree queries blocked for performance Recommended: Themes/Initiatives at Workspace root (or any ancestor); Path C clamp predicate at view layer
Lifecycle / delete Cannot delete; Close (= soft archive); children must close first; reopen requires parents open Archive supported Delete + transfer supported; visibility re-checks on transfer Archive/delete supported; team-managed deletable Delete supported with recycle bin restore Soft-archive (matches existing Vector tenant pattern); cannot delete with active descendants

4. What Vector Already Does Right

The following Vector decisions are already aligned with — or ahead of — industry-leading practice as documented in R022–R027:

  • Single-table self-referential FK for the strategy chain. Mirrors GitLab's unified issues table and Jira's single Issue store; cleaner than Targetprocess's per-type tables (which forces N-way joins for any cross-level traversal) and simpler than Azure DevOps's link-record-in-link-store model (which requires walking a generic link graph to recover hierarchy).
  • Strict five-level chain. Targetprocess and Azure DevOps are the only two competitors that lock the chain; Rally, GitLab, and Jira all let admins inject custom levels at runtime (which is a UX feature but a correctness liability — Jira's docs explicitly warn that adding a hierarchy level orphans every existing top-level item).
  • Async rollups with reconciliation timestamp. Matches Rally's LastRollupDate pattern and Azure's Analytics-service projection — both of which are the published-best models for large hierarchies. Strictly better than Targetprocess's compute-on-read (which doesn't scale past mid-size trees) and simpler than Jira/GitLab's "live but limited" approach.
  • Recursive CTE for subtree traversal. Standard and correct for self-referential trees; Azure DevOps, GitLab, and Jira all use equivalent recursive walks at query time.
  • Type-aware drop predicate from a registry. Azure DevOps does this implicitly via category rules ("you can only reparent backlog items under other features, and features under other epics") — Vector formalising it as an explicit registry-driven predicate is the cleanest design move in this space.
  • Subtree-moves-as-unit on reparent. GitLab's child-items model behaves this way; Azure DevOps does NOT (it leaves children in their original Project, creating cross-project parent edges) and this is a documented pain point. Vector's choice is the correct one.
  • Rejected closure tables. Closure tables are write-amplifying (every reparent touches O(depth × subtree-size) rows); the recursive-CTE-on-read model used by every modern tool in this comparison is the right call. Vector skips a known-bad architecture.
  • Rejected polymorphic FK via entityrefs. Self-referential type-strict FK is more correct than entityrefs because it enforces the chain at the schema level — entityrefs are a runtime contract, the schema is a compile-time contract.

5. What Vector Does Better Than the Field

Three areas where Vector's current architecture beats every tool surveyed:

  • Orphans-by-design impossibility. Every other tool surveyed permits orphans — Rally has an opt-in workspace setting, Targetprocess flags but allows them, GitLab/Jira/Azure all permit and surface them as a UX category. Vector's NOT NULL constraint on the parent FK at every non-root level makes orphans structurally impossible. This is the single most defensible correctness claim Vector has against the field.
  • Cycles structurally impossible by type-asymmetry. Each child type has exactly one legal parent type (Initiative→Theme, Feature→Initiative, UserStory→Feature, Task→UserStory). A cycle would require a Feature to parent a Theme, which the type registry rejects. This is stronger than GitLab's runtime cycle check or Azure DevOps's "tree topology link" assertion — those rely on enforcement code; Vector relies on the type system.
  • Single-FK-per-table simplicity. Azure DevOps stores the parent in a generic link-store row alongside Related/Predecessor/Tested-By links — five+ link types in the same table. GitLab uses a separate work_item_parent_links table with extra schema. Targetprocess scatters parent FKs across N type-tables. Vector's two columns (one per table) is the smallest correct surface.

6. Gaps — What Industry Leaders Have That Vector Lacks

The following capabilities exist in one or more competitors and are absent (or unfinished) in Vector. Each row names the best implementation in the field and a concrete recommendation.

Gap Best implementation observed Vector recommendation
Hierarchy-range filter on backlog views (From/To level) Jira Plans — pick "show levels Initiative through User Story"; full hierarchy toggle ensures children of matches appear Add level_min / level_max URL params to the recursive-CTE query and a control bar on the backlog view; clamp at any of the 5 levels
Cross-Workspace strategy view Rally — PortfolioItems at Workspace level above Projects, span all descendants Path C: tree is global; Workspace is a clamp predicate at view time, not a hard partition (see §8)
Drag-and-drop reparent in the tree view GitLab — drag-and-drop child-items UI; Azure DevOps — drag-reparent on backlog Parents view + multi-select Change Parent Wire the existing type-aware drop predicate to a tree drag handler; show drop-target highlight only on legal targets; multi-select bulk reparent action menu
What-if / sandbox planning workspace Jira Plans — sandbox for date adjustments, team assignments, dependency mapping; only writes back to live data on explicit Save Defer to phase 2; add a plan_session table that snapshots a subtree and lets the user diff-and-commit; not blocking for v1
Roadmap / Gantt view of subtree GitLab Roadmap — Gantt rows expand-collapse; "filter to descendants" action; Jira Timeline; Azure DevOps Delivery Plans Add a Gantt view backed by the recursive CTE + start/end date fields on portfolio items; expand/collapse rows; hover for progress
Custom-named hierarchy levels per Workspace Rally — workspace admins rename Theme/Initiative/Feature; Azure DevOps — inheritance process renames; Targetprocess — per-process term rename Per-Workspace label override table (display only — schema labels stay as Theme/Initiative/Feature/UserStory/Task internally); gadmin-only setting
Tenant-configurable minimum-attach-level None of the surveyed tools — Vector original Per-Workspace setting "items at/below level N must specify a Workspace" — defaults at Feature; gadmin-only
Attached-from-elsewhere link (the "paste full URL" pattern) GitLab Epics — paste full issue URL to add an issue from a different group hierarchy; surfaces as link without changing parent Add a separate related_links table for non-tree relations (does NOT overload the parent FK); keeps strict tree intact
Burndown / progress on each subtree node GitLab Epic header — descendant count + total weight + progress %; Azure DevOps rollup columns Surface last_rollup_at + computed completion % + descendant count on every Theme/Initiative/Feature card; refresh button for manual reconciliation when stale
Audit "show me orphans" / "items whose Workspace differs from parent" Azure DevOps — built-in audit prompts for Area Path drift between parent and child Vector's NOT NULL kills the orphan-audit need; add a "Workspace drift" report (list items whose workspace_id differs from their parent's) for Path C scoping

7. Recommendations for Market-Leading Vector

Concrete, implementation-ready recommendations grouped by priority. Each item includes the source tool that established the pattern and the schema/UX move required.

  1. Adopt Path C scoping (global tree, container-clamped at view time). See §8 for the full case. Schema move: workspace_id nullable on portfolio_items and user_stories; nullable for top-level Themes/Initiatives that span the entire tenant; required at and below the level the Workspace setting designates as the minimum-attach-level. Source: Rally's PortfolioItem-at-Workspace-level pattern.
  2. Adopt the name "Workspace" for the scoping container. Aha! and Linear both use it deliberately to avoid the time-bounded-initiative connotation of "Project"; the user explicitly hates "Project". Schema move: workspaces table with self-referential FK parent_workspace_id (subgroup-style nesting up to a sane depth like 5–10).
  3. Add hierarchy-range filtering to backlog and tree views. URL params level_min / level_max; bind the recursive CTE to them; render a chip-strip at the top of the view. Source: Jira Plans hierarchy-band filter.
  4. Wire drag-and-drop reparent through the type-aware predicate. Drop targets light up only when the registry's can_parent(child_type, parent_type) returns true. Multi-select + bulk Change Parent action. Source: Azure DevOps backlog drag-reparent + GitLab child-items drag.
  5. Surface last_rollup_at as a freshness indicator. Show on every Theme/Initiative/Feature card; show a refresh icon if the stamp is older than N minutes. Source: Rally's "Correct rollup discrepancy" affordance + Azure's Analytics latency UI.
  6. Per-Workspace custom labels (display only). A workspace_label_overrides table maps internal names (theme, initiative, feature, user_story, task) to tenant display names. Schema and code never see the override — pure presentation layer. Source: Rally workspace admin rename + Targetprocess per-process terms.
  7. Add a related_links table for non-tree relations. Blocks, duplicates, references — these belong on a separate edge table, NOT in the parent FK. Keeps the strict tree intact while supporting the GitLab "paste URL to attach" pattern and Azure DevOps's Related / Predecessor / Tested-By link types.
  8. Add a Gantt / Roadmap view backed by the recursive CTE. Expand-collapse subtree rows; hover for progress; date range from start_date / end_date fields on portfolio items (add columns if not present). Source: GitLab Roadmap, Azure DevOps Delivery Plans, Jira Timeline.
  9. Add Workspace permissions cascade with the GitLab pattern. User role on Workspace inherits to all descendant Workspaces and items; explicit grants override; minimum-access role for "see the Workspace exists, no item access". Source: GitLab subgroup inheritance + Jira project-role model.
  10. Defer plan-sandbox until v2. Jira Plans sandbox is high-value but high-cost; Vector should ship the strict-tree + recommendations 1–9 first, then evaluate.
  11. Add an "audit drift" report. List items whose workspace_id differs from their parent's. With Path C, drift is allowed (children can move workspaces) but should be visible. Source: Azure DevOps Area Path drift audit prompts.
  12. Document the strict-tree-by-construction guarantee in onboarding copy. This is Vector's principal differentiator — every other tool tells users to "watch out for orphans"; Vector says "orphans are impossible". Lean into the marketing.

8. Open Architectural Decisions — Resolved

Decision A: Path A (single global tree) vs Path B (container-scoped tree) vs Path C (hybrid: global tree clamped by container at view time)

Recommendation: Path C.

The cross-tool evidence is unambiguous on this point. Rally explicitly decoupled strategy (PortfolioItems at Workspace level) from execution (Projects below) precisely because forcing a strategic item into a single delivery container produces miscategorisation pain at scale. GitLab does the same with Epics-at-Group. Jira added Plans (cross-project sandbox) because users complained that the project-bounded model couldn't express programme-level work. Azure DevOps recommends "single project + Area Paths" because cross-project work loses history.

Path A (single global tree, no containers) is what Vector has today and is correct but doesn't scale to multi-team tenants. Path B (container-scoped tree, every item lives in exactly one container) is what Jira does — and the entire Plans product exists because it's wrong for strategic work. Path C — tree is global, every item has an optional workspace_id that clamps the view but does not partition the storage — is what Rally + GitLab + Linear converge on, and it's what Vector should adopt.

Implementation: workspace_id nullable on portfolio_items and user_stories; recursive CTE accepts an optional clamp predicate; cross-workspace reparenting allowed (subtree-moves-as-unit rewrites workspace_id on every descendant); audit drift report flags children whose workspace_id ≠ parent's workspace_id (allowed but visible).

Decision A.2: Path C clarified — the container is itself a tree

The original Path C framing ("global tree, Workspace as a clamp predicate at view time") was incomplete. It did not specify whether the Workspace container is itself a tree or a flat tag. The user's clarification is unambiguous: the container is a tree, mirroring how real organisations are actually structured.

Schema. An org_nodes table with a self-referential parent_id, the same pattern Vector already uses for portfolio_items.hierarchy_parent. Arbitrary depth, arbitrary width per branch. workspace_id on items becomes org_node_id (or equivalent) and points at any level of the org tree.

Scale grounding. A Lloyds-scale tenant might have 1,000–3,000 nodes (Group → Division → Sub-division → Department → Team → Squad). Depth and width vary per branch — the Retail Bank may have eight levels and 200 leaf teams; an Insurance arm three levels and a dozen. Any model that fixes the levels or caps the depth is wrong at this scale.

Levels are not fixed. Vector does not impose a taxonomy. The gadmin names the levels themselves — Department/Team, Tribe/Squad, Division/Pod, Function/Cell — whatever vocabulary the tenant uses. Stored as a per-tenant level dictionary; rendered in the canvas as the tenant's own labels.

Clamp policy is per-node, not global. Each org node carries its own clamp policy: inherit (default; take parent's), open (no clamp — items below this node are visible across the subtree), restrict-subtree (clamp this node and everything below), restrict-node (clamp this node only; children re-open). The per-Workspace min_attach_level from Decision C becomes per-Node, allowing a Group-level Workspace to admit Themes globally while a specific Department clamps tighter.

Org Design is a product surface, not a settings form. This is the load-bearing point. Rally's "Project" failed for two reasons: (a) the noun was wrong — Barclays trainees spent twenty minutes per session arguing about whether a thing was a Project or not — and (b) the surface was a settings list, not a designed artefact, so nobody treated org structure as a thing worth designing. Vector flips both. The page is a canvas-based drag-and-drop block-diagram editor — boxes, connectors, collapse/expand, minimap. Org structure becomes an artefact gadmin (and ideally padmin) can shape, version, and review, not a hidden hierarchy buried in admin.

Decouple structure from permission. A user belongs to a Node for organisational reasons; a user has a clamp anchor for policy reasons. 90% of the time these coincide and the system can default the clamp anchor from the node membership. The remaining 10% — a director who sits in Engineering but needs cross-company visibility, a programme lead with multi-division scope, a contractor attached to one team but billable to another — is supported by allowing the clamp anchor to differ from the node membership without distorting the org tree itself. Two columns on the user/membership join, not two trees.

Scale constraints on the canvas. At 1,000+ nodes, a flat canvas is unusable. Required at enterprise scale (not optional): collapse-by-default with lazy expand on click; minimap for spatial navigation; breadcrumb trail anchored to the currently expanded path; full-text search across node names with jump-to-node; auto-layout (dagre or elk) with a per-node manual position override stored on the node row. Without these the canvas dies above ~200 nodes.

Naming — still open. The page name and the node noun are not yet decided. Working candidates: page = "Org Design"; node = Unit, Group, Branch, Node, or gadmin-named (each tenant picks its own root noun). The user has explicitly raised this as an open decision; do not lock in either name in code or schema until it is settled.

Positioning — open strategic question. At enterprise scale (1,000+ nodes, versioning, what-if org redesigns, exportable diagrams) Org Design begins to overlap with the enterprise-architecture tool market — Ardoq, LeanIX, Sparx EA, BizzDesign. Whether Vector treats Org Design as a scoping primitive that exists only to support its work-management product, or as a first-class EA-grade artefact in its own right, is a strategic decision still open. The schema described above supports both paths; the UX surface diverges substantially (a scoping primitive needs a competent canvas; an EA-grade tool needs change history, impact analysis, ArchiMate-style metamodel, and integrations). Flag for the product roadmap; do not let the answer be implicit.

Decision B: Container naming — Workspace vs Domain vs Bucket vs Project vs Area vs Group

Recommendation: Workspace.

The field falls into two camps. The "Project" camp (Rally, Targetprocess, Jira, Azure DevOps) inherited the term from Microsoft Project / project-management heritage, and every one of them has documentation pages explaining that the term is misleading because the container is long-lived, not bounded by a deadline (Rally docs literally say "a project just means a node of your organizational structure which represents a team"). The user's stated dislike of "Project" matches this widely documented pain.

The "non-Project" camp (Aha!, Linear, GitLab) chose alternatives:

  • Aha! — uses Workspace; reserves "Project" for one workspace type alongside Product, IT, Marketing, Services. Workspaces nest via "workspace lines".
  • Linear — uses Team at the persistent level; Project is a separate, time-bounded concept.
  • GitLab — uses Group + Subgroup, with Project still reserved for the code+CI unit at the bottom.

"Workspace" wins on three grounds: (1) Aha! has the clearest case for it as the long-lived organisational container (matching Vector's intent); (2) it is already industry-recognised vocabulary that doesn't need user education; (3) it sidesteps the user's explicit objection to "Project". "Domain" and "Bucket" are not used by any major tool in this comparison and would require explanation. "Group" is overloaded with permission-group connotations. "Area" collides with Azure DevOps's Area Path which is a different concept.

Caveat — node-level noun and page name still open. "Workspace" remains the recommended name for the high-level container, but once Decision A.2 is accepted the org tree has many node levels, and the noun for an individual node (Unit, Group, Branch, Node, or gadmin-named per tenant) plus the name of the design surface itself (working name "Org Design") have not been decided. The user has flagged both as open. Treat them as live decisions, not settled defaults.

Decision C: Clamp level granularity — clamp at any level vs clamp only at Theme/Initiative

Recommendation: Clamp at any level, governed by a per-Workspace minimum-attach-level setting.

The cross-tool evidence here is more split. Rally and GitLab both clamp at the strategic level (PortfolioItems above containers, Epics at Group). Azure DevOps clamps inside via Area Paths. Targetprocess clamps everything at Project. Jira clamps at Project for everything except Plans.

Forcing the clamp at only Theme/Initiative (the strict Rally/GitLab pattern) is the simplest design and matches the strategy/execution decoupling argument cleanly — but it is too rigid for tenants who want their Themes themselves to be Workspace-scoped. Forcing the clamp at every level (strict Targetprocess pattern) is what users complained about — it forces strategic items to live inside one execution silo.

The right answer is to make the clamp level a tenant-configurable setting on the Workspace itself, defaulting to Feature: above Feature (Themes, Initiatives) is global by default; at Feature and below (User Stories, Tasks) requires a workspace_id. A gadmin can change the default for a Workspace to "clamp at any level" (every item under this Workspace must have workspace_id set) or "clamp at Initiative" (Initiatives and below carry Workspace; Themes are global). This is a single column on the workspaces table — min_attach_level with values theme/initiative/feature/user_story/task — and the recursive-CTE clamp predicate honours it.

This produces the "tenant-configurable container model is a possible fourth dimension" outcome the user already flagged: gadmin-level setting, no schema change beyond the one column, and per-Workspace flexibility.

Decision A.3: Build the canvas substrate ourselves — expose it via Samantha API

R029's library evaluation showed no third-party canvas hits all three of our constraints (3,000-node performance, MIT-class licence, HTML-in-nodes). React Flow has documented scale cliffs; GoJS is commercial-only with a licence forbidding derivative works (£15k–£40k year-one for a SaaS team — same as the engineering effort to build it); Cytoscape.js is fast enough but cannot host arbitrary HTML in nodes.

Decision: build a Vector-owned <DiagramCanvas> primitive on Canvas2D + dagre (MIT) layout in a Web Worker + d3-zoom (BSD) for pan/zoom. We need ~5% of what a general-purpose graph library provides (rectangles, parent-child connectors, drag-drop, hit-test, minimap) — most of GoJS is bloat for our use case. Estimated build: 3–5 weeks for one senior engineer. Cytoscape.js remains as a documented stopgap if Phase 1 slips beyond 6 weeks.

Strategic upside: the primitive is exposed as samantha.diagram.canvas in the Samantha API. Custom-app and custom-page authors can render their own diagrams (process flows, dependency maps, customer journeys, capability overlays) using the same primitive Org Design uses. The investment pays back across every future visualisation feature, with no third-party licence drag.

This decision is captured in PLA-0006 (Org Design plan); R029 is the evidence base.
